fields.description:
# Invalidate downstream plan reviews after upstream plan-material changes

## Problem
A plan-review attestation can remain valid after a direct child or prerequisite changes its plan. In a large epic or dependency chain, an agent can therefore claim or close work using assumptions that no longer match the plans it relies on. The existing material fingerprint protects only the reviewed ticket itself; it does not bind the reviewed plan to the current material of related tickets.

## Outcome
Every new plan review records signed plan-material pins for the ticket's direct children and direct prerequisites. A pin binds the related ticket's canonical ID, its role, and its current narrow material fingerprint. When enforcement is enabled, material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation. Propagation is deliberately natural rather than eager: an upstream change invalidates its direct dependent; farther downstream tickets invalidate only if the intermediate ticket adapts materially.

## Why this approach
Signed pins extend the existing computed-on-read attestation-validity model: the downstream review cryptographically records exactly what related plan material it relied on, while later reads compare that evidence without writing graph-wide invalidation events. This avoids tickets-branch write amplification, introduces no new storage primitive, and permits legacy attestations and older readers to continue by ignoring additive metadata. Natural propagation also preserves the causal boundary: a downstream plan is reconsidered only when an intermediate plan actually changes.

## Settled design

### Material and relationships
- Material remains the current narrow definition: description and acceptance criteria, file impact, and direct-child IDs.
- Attestation validity is not propagated. A related ticket's review can be invalid without affecting downstream tickets when its material did not change.
- Direct prerequisites are the union of outgoing depends_on targets and inbound blocks sources. Stored relation types are unchanged; aliases and short IDs are resolved before use.
- Parent/child review behavior already exists and is not changed by this epic. Children participate only in material pinning.

### Signed plan-material pins
- New plan reviews encode each pin as the signed manifest line plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>. The only role strings are child and prerequisite. Lines sort by role then canonical ID. A ticket qualifying for both roles has two pins; duplicate graph edges within a role collapse to one pin. Unknown roles, malformed fields, or conflicting duplicate values are invalid.
- Fingerprints use the existing narrow material fingerprint format. Pin collection is all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets prevent signing. Oversized LLM context is separate: pin collection may succeed, but the too-large review finding prevents a PASS and therefore prevents signing.
- Pins are automatic signed attestation metadata. There is no new CLI or MCP operation and no first-order ticket field.
- Legacy attestations without pins remain valid. No migration or eager backfill is performed; the next ordinary re-review adds pins.
- Archived tickets remain readable. A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review.

### Enforcement and rollout
- verify.enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false. Pin drift remains visible but advisory while disabled; valid remains true.
- Re-enabling enforcement immediately applies existing pin-aware attestations. It never rewrites pins, while legacy unpinned attestations continue to pass. Pin lines are additive manifest metadata: readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines and therefore treat the attestation like a legacy unpinned review. Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.
- Pin validity uses one implementation wherever an attestation is already being evaluated: claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health. The pin flag does not independently activate claim or close gates. At close, pin drift can block only when both require_plan_review_for_close and enforce_plan_material_pins are enabled; otherwise observed drift is advisory.
- New reviews emit pins in every project regardless of enforcement configuration. The rebar project enables enforcement.

### Execution reviews and close gate
- Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots; it is not a user-editable ticket field or a new event type. Every resolved transition into in_progress sets it true. Every resolved transition into open clears it. Other statuses preserve it. Reviews read this O(1) projection, never scan raw history, so compacted tickets behave identically. Example: open -> in_progress -> blocked remains execution; blocked -> open resets planning.
- The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80. A syntactically valid floor is numeric in [0.0, 1.0]; an execution attestation is compatible only at >= 0.80. Absence of review-phase is the legacy planning value; missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed.
- Planning mode accepts only planning. Execution mode accepts planning (the stricter full review) or execution with priority-floor numerically at least 0.80; lower or malformed execution floors are incompatible.
- During execution, each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.80). Advisory-only criteria stay advisory, deterministic structural failures stay blocking, and lower-priority findings remain visible. The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config. The numeric manifest field is evolution-safe: a future required floor compares numerically, so raising policy makes older execution attestations require re-review while lowering it accepts attestations carrying the older higher floor; no manifest-format migration is required.
- verify.require_plan_review_for_close defaults to false. When enabled, task, story, and epic close operations require a current certified plan review. Bugs, session logs, code reviews, identities, and idea-to-closed transitions remain exempt.
- Close performs no LLM review. A failed gate directs the agent to run review-plan separately.
- Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins, and other non-code attestation validity, but intentionally ignores file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD freshness because implementation normally changes impacted files.
- The existing force-close with a non-empty reason bypasses completion and close-time plan-review gates with an audit record. Structural child-closure rules remain non-bypassable.
- Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing. Close prechecks before completion verification and rechecks under the store write lock before writing STATUS.

### Prerequisite consistency review
- A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General reviewers do not receive prerequisite material. The review snapshot preloads compiled ticket states once and reuses that map for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery, avoiding a separate full-store reduction for every prerequisite.
- The criterion receives the subject plan and the current narrow material of each prerequisite; existing parent/child criteria and prompts are unchanged.
- Multiple prerequisite evaluations may share a call when whole prerequisite blocks fit within existing context headroom. Batching is an optimization only: each evaluation asks whether the subject is consistent with one prerequisite.
- Pass 1 returns exactly one coverage record per canonical prerequisite: {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}, where disposition is consistent, finding, or indeterminate. consistent requires an empty findings list; finding requires one or more candidate findings all attributed to that same prerequisite_id; indeterminate records why evaluation or attribution could not be completed and cannot block. Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, or schema-invalid records become indeterminate rather than a clean pass.
- Pass 2 receives the same subject/prerequisite map and verifies substance and prerequisite_id attribution. Only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3 and the execution floor; unattributed or cross-ID findings cannot block.
- Context-limit failures retry with smaller bins. Subject plus one oversized prerequisite yields the existing too-large remediation.
- Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory. Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing.

### Health and documentation
- Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI. Ordinary list output remains unchanged.
- Health is derived at read time; no warning event is persisted. When enforcement is off, the UI labels drift advisory.
- plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Every new passing plan review signs a complete deterministic pin set.
- [ ] Pin coverage includes every direct child.
- [ ] Pin coverage includes every normalized direct prerequisite.
- [ ] Every pin uses an authoritative canonical ticket ID.
- [ ] Every pin uses exactly the child or prerequisite role.
- [ ] Every pin uses the existing narrow material fingerprint.
- [ ] Legacy unpinned attestations remain valid.
- [ ] Readers predating the feature safely ignore additive pin lines.
- [ ] Enabled pin enforcement invalidates all named attestation consumers after related material drift.
- [ ] Disabled pin enforcement preserves attestation validity while reporting advisory drift.
- [ ] The reducer projection survives compaction with identical execution-mode behavior.
- [ ] Entry into in_progress establishes execution mode.
- [ ] Entry into open resets planning mode.
- [ ] Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor.
- [ ] Execution reviews keep sub-floor findings visible.
- [ ] Deterministic structural failures remain blocking during execution.
- [ ] Enabled close enforcement requires a separately earned phase-compatible review.
- [ ] Code freshness alone never invalidates the close-profile plan review.
- [ ] Close exemptions match the documented ticket types.
- [ ] Audited force-close bypasses both LLM-backed close gates.
- [ ] Structural child closure remains non-bypassable.
- [ ] Every prerequisite receives exactly one three-disposition coverage record.
- [ ] Every blocking prerequisite finding carries one verified canonical prerequisite ID.
- [ ] Packed prerequisite recall is no lower than the one-per-call gold baseline.
- [ ] Packed prerequisite false acceptance is no higher than the gold baseline.
- [ ] Packed prerequisite attribution errors equal zero.
- [ ] Detailed CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, and web surfaces expose the same derived health contract.
- [ ] Ordinary list output remains unchanged.
- [ ] Rebar enables both enforcement flags while reusable defaults remain disabled.
- [ ] Public lifecycle tests prove toggling, legacy compatibility, natural transitivity, deletion, archive handling, execution adaptation, concurrency, and force-close behavior.

## Not in scope
- Consolidating or migrating depends_on and blocks storage.
- Changing existing parent/child review criteria or injecting relationship context into every reviewer.
- Eager graph-wide or transitive invalidation events.
- Persisted invalidation warnings or a new user-facing pin command.
- Backfilling or rewriting existing attestations.
